title: Access Level Sizing Guide
summary: Use this guide to understand what the Access Level Sizing tool is checking, how to think about the inputs, and how to interpret the result.

sections.title: What this tool does
body: This tool estimates the structural complexity of an access-control design. It is not just counting doors. It looks at how roles, areas, schedules, door groups, concurrency, and operational rules combine into a system that may be easy to manage or hard to maintain.
The goal is to flag when an access model is starting to become fragile, over-complicated, or likely to create administrative mistakes.
bullets: Use it before building access levels in a platform.
Use it when comparing simple role-based access against more granular segmented access.
Use it to explain why a design may need simplification before deployment.

sections.title: When to use it
body: Use this tool during early design, expansion planning, or cleanup of an existing access-control system. It is especially useful when a site has multiple departments, different operating schedules, restricted areas, or frequent user changes.
bullets: New building or campus access planning.
Door expansion projects.
Role cleanup after years of accumulated access levels.
Comparing a simple access model against a highly segmented model.

sections.title: Input descriptions
body: These descriptions explain what each input represents in practical access-control terms.

items.label: Roles
items.description: A role is a group of users with similar access needs, such as employees, managers, cleaning staff, warehouse staff, or IT personnel.
items.whyItMatters: More roles usually means more access combinations to manage and audit.
items.commonMistake: Creating a new role for every small exception instead of grouping users by real operational need.

items.label: Areas
items.description: An area is a logical zone or part of the site that needs separate access treatment.
items.whyItMatters: More areas can increase the number of role-area combinations needed in the access model.
items.commonMistake: Breaking the building into too many tiny zones when a simpler grouping would be easier to maintain.

items.label: Door Groups
items.description: A door group is a set of doors managed together because they share similar access behavior.
items.whyItMatters: More door groups usually means more permission combinations to manage, document, and troubleshoot.
items.commonMistake: Creating a separate door group for every small exception instead of grouping doors by real operational purpose.

items.label: Schedules
items.description: A schedule is a time window that controls when access is allowed or restricted.
items.whyItMatters: Schedules add another layer of logic. Too many schedules can make access behavior harder to predict and audit.
items.commonMistake: Using many overlapping schedules instead of standardizing around a few clear operating windows.

items.label: Concurrency
items.description: Concurrency represents how many users or access cases may need to operate under the same access model at the same time.
items.whyItMatters: Higher concurrency can make mistakes more visible because more people depend on the same access structure working correctly.
items.commonMistake: Designing access rules for a small test group without considering how the model behaves at full operating scale.

items.label: Special Rules
items.description: Special rules are exceptions or restrictions that do not fit the normal access pattern.
items.whyItMatters: Exceptions are often where access systems become hard to support. A few are normal; too many can create long-term administrative risk.
items.commonMistake: Solving every exception with a permanent access rule instead of keeping the baseline clean and documenting exceptions separately.

sections.title: How to read the results
body: A healthy result usually means the access model is manageable and likely to be understandable by admins. A watch result means the system may still work, but the design deserves review before implementation. A risk result means the structure may become difficult to maintain, audit, or troubleshoot.
bullets: Healthy: clean model, manageable structure, low administrative friction.
Watch: workable, but review grouping, schedules, and exceptions.
Risk: simplify the design or split the problem into clearer zones, roles, or phases.

sections.title: Common mistakes
body: The most common mistake is treating every small access difference as a new access level. That can create a system that works on paper but becomes painful to administer.
bullets: Creating too many one-off access levels.
Mixing departments, schedules, and exceptions into the same access group.
Designing for every edge case instead of building a clean baseline and handling exceptions separately.
Forgetting that someone has to audit and maintain the system later.

sections.title: Planning assumptions
body: This tool assumes complexity increases as roles, areas, schedules, and grouped permissions multiply. It does not know the exact rules of every access-control platform, so the result should be treated as planning guidance rather than a manufacturer-specific limit.
bullets: Manufacturer limits still matter.
Platform licensing, controller capacity, and credential rules may impose separate constraints.
A simpler model is usually easier to support, document, and audit.

sections.title: Professional responsibility
body: ScopedLabs tools are planning aids. They help surface assumptions and risk, but they do not replace formal engineering review, code compliance review, manufacturer validation, or project-specific design responsibility.
bullets: Validate against the actual access-control platform.
Confirm project requirements and owner expectations.
Document assumptions before implementation.
